# latency-lab

> A zero-runtime-dependency TypeScript toolkit for simulating network latency,
> jitter, packet loss, TCP drops, and HTTP failures during development and
> automated testing.

latency-lab supports outbound Fetch interception in browsers and Node.js,
server middleware for Express, Next.js, Fastify, and Hono, and a zero-code
HTTP/HTTPS reverse proxy for applications written in any language. It requires
Node.js 18 or newer and is not intended for production traffic.

## Canonical documentation

- [README and API reference](https://github.com/MaheshTrapasiya/latency-lab#readme)
- [npm package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/latency-lab)
- [Changelog](https://github.com/MaheshTrapasiya/latency-lab/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md)
- [Examples](https://github.com/MaheshTrapasiya/latency-lab/tree/main/examples)
- [Security policy](https://github.com/MaheshTrapasiya/latency-lab/blob/main/SECURITY.md)

## Package entry points

- `latency-lab`: core chaos engine, presets, Express middleware, and Fetch APIs
- `latency-lab/fetch`: `createChaosFetch`, `installFetchChaos`, `ChaosFetchError`
- `latency-lab/express`: Express and Connect middleware
- `latency-lab/next`: Next.js App Router handler wrapper
- `latency-lab/fastify`: Fastify `onRequest` hook
- `latency-lab/hono`: Hono middleware
- `latency-lab/core`: validation, delay, and failure decision utilities
- `latency-lab/presets`: reusable degraded-network profiles
- `npx latency-lab`: language-agnostic HTTP/HTTPS chaos proxy

## Common tasks

- Simulate slow outgoing API calls with `createChaosFetch()`.
- Temporarily intercept global Fetch with `installFetchChaos()` and restore it
  after each test.
- Inject latency and failures into incoming framework requests with an adapter.
- Put the CLI reverse proxy in front of a local service without modifying code.
- Use URL and route exclusions to keep health checks unaffected.
- Use route inclusions and per-route overrides to target only selected APIs.
- Share typed `ChaosScenario` objects across team config files.
- Use abort signals to test timeout and cancellation behavior during delays.

## Behavioral guarantees

- HTTP failures return synthetic JSON responses.
- Fetch TCP drops reject with `ChaosFetchError` and `ERR_CHAOS_TCP_DROP`.
- Fetch abort signals are respected during injected delays.
- The CLI streams HTTP methods, request bodies, response bodies, and headers.
- ESM, CommonJS, strict TypeScript declarations, and Node.js 18+ are supported.
- The package has zero runtime dependencies.
- Middleware route precedence is exclusions, per-route overrides, inclusions,
  then the default chaos options.

## Out of scope

- Direct `undici.request()` interception
- WebSocket proxying
- Production traffic degradation
